html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Nine — Part One

The Book of Tehillim

TEHILLIM

The Psalms — The Praises of Yisra'EL

Psalms 1 through 20

The Songs of Dawid — Book I Opening

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

11Q5 (11QPsa) — Cave 11 — Major Psalms Scroll — Ps 101-150 + Ps 151, 154, 155

4Q83 (4QPsa) — Cave 4 — Psalms 5-71 fragments

4Q85 (4QPsc) — Cave 4 — Psalms 16, 18, 27

8Q2 (8QPsa) — Cave 8 — Psalms 17-18

3Q2 (3QPsa) — Cave 3 — Psalm 2:6-7

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon — All 150 Psalms plus Psalm 151

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

BOOK I — PSALMS 1 THROUGH 41

TEHILLIM — PSALM 1 The Two Ways — The Righteous and the Wicked

1 Blessed is the man who walks not in the counsel of the wicked, nor stands in the way of sinners, nor sits in the seat of scoffers.

2 But his delight is in the Torah of YHWH, and on his Torah he meditates day and night.

3 He is like a tree planted by streams of water that yields its fruit in its season, and its leaf does not wither. In all that he does, he prospers.

4 The wicked are not so, but are like chaff that the wind drives away.

5 Therefore the wicked will not stand in the judgment, nor sinners in the congregation of the righteous.

6 For YHWH knows the way of the righteous, but the way of the wicked will perish.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 1 is attested in fragmentary form across multiple Cave 4 manuscripts including 4QPsa. The text is substantially consistent with the Masoretic in all preserved portions. Psalm 1 serves as the gateway to the entire Tehillim collection — it has no superscription and functions as an introduction to the whole.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 1 in full as part of the canonical Tehillim. The Ethiopian Beta Israel tradition treats the Psalms as sacred scripture read in liturgical worship.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 1. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading throughout.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 1 opens the entire Tehillim collection with the foundational covenant principle: two ways, two destinies. The way of the righteous — those who meditate on YHWH's Torah day and night — and the way of the wicked who will perish. The tree planted by streams of water is the covenant man who draws sustenance from YHWH's word. The chaff driven by wind is the man with no root. This binary frames the entire book of Tehillim — every psalm is addressed to or speaks about one of these two types of human being.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 2 The Reign of YHWH and His Anointed Over the Nations

1 Why do the nations rage and the peoples plot in vain?

2 The kings of the earth set themselves, and the rulers take counsel together, against YHWH and against his Anointed, saying,

3 Let us burst their bonds apart and cast away their cords from us.

4 He who sits in the heavens laughs — YHWH holds them in derision.

5 Then he will speak to them in his wrath and terrify them in his fury, saying,

6 As for me, I have set my King on Tziyon, my holy hill.

7 I will tell of the decree: YHWH said to me, You are my son — today I have begotten you.

8 Ask of me, and I will make the nations your heritage, and the ends of the earth your possession.

9 You shall break them with a rod of iron and dash them in pieces like a potter's vessel.

10 Now therefore, O kings, be wise — be warned, O rulers of the earth.

11 Serve YHWH with fear, and rejoice with trembling.

12 Blessed are all who take refuge in him.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 2 is partially preserved in 3QPsa (3Q2) which contains Psalm 2:6-7. The fragment confirms the reading of the key verse — I have set my King on Tziyon. 4QPsa also preserves portions of this psalm. The text is consistent with the Masoretic reading in all preserved fragments.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 2 in full. The psalm is read as a covenant declaration of YHWH's sovereignty over all earthly kingdoms through his Anointed — his Mashiakh — seated on Tziyon.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in preserved portions of Psalm 2. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS fragments.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — YHWH said to me, You are my son — today I have begotten you. This is the royal adoption formula of the Davidic covenant — the king of Yisra'EL declared the son of YHWH upon his coronation. The decree referred to is the covenant of 2 Shemu'el 7 — YHWH's covenant with Dawid that his throne would endure. The nations raging against YHWH's Anointed is not merely a historical observation — it is the permanent condition of the world until YHWH's sovereignty is fully established. The psalm closes with the invitation: blessed are all who take refuge in him — extending the covenant hope beyond Yisra'EL to anyone who submits to YHWH's reign.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 3 Of Dawid — When He Fled From Avshalom His Son — YHWH as Shield

1 YHWH, how many are my foes — many are rising against me.

2 Many are saying of my soul, There is no salvation for him in YHWH.

3 But you, YHWH, are a shield about me, my glory, and the lifter of my head.

4 I cried aloud to YHWH, and he answered me from his holy hill.

5 I lay down and slept — I woke again, for YHWH sustained me.

6 I will not be afraid of many thousands of people who have set themselves against me all around.

7 Arise, YHWH — save me, O my Creator — for you strike all my enemies on the cheek — you break the teeth of the wicked.

8 Salvation belongs to YHWH — your blessing be on your people.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 3 is attested in 4QPsa with fragmentary coverage. The superscription identifying the historical context as Dawid's flight from Avshalom is present in the DSS tradition. The text aligns with the Masoretic reading in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 3 in full with the superscription intact. The historical superscription — connecting the psalm to Dawid's flight from his son Avshalom — is preserved as part of the canonical text.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 3. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading throughout.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — you, YHWH, are a shield about me, my glory, and the lifter of my head. Three attributes in one verse: YHWH as protective enclosure, YHWH as the source of the psalmist's honor, and YHWH as the one who raises the bowed head. The bowing of the head in shame and grief is reversed by YHWH's intervention. Verse 5 — I lay down and slept — I woke again, for YHWH sustained me. Sleep in the context of mortal danger is itself an act of trust. The ability to sleep while surrounded by thousands of enemies is not courage — it is faith in YHWH as the sustainer through the night.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 4 Of Dawid — Evening Prayer — Trust in YHWH Alone

1 Answer me when I call, O Creator of my righteousness — you have given me relief when I was in distress. Be gracious to me and hear my prayer.

2 O men, how long shall my honor be turned into shame — how long will you love vain words and seek after lies?

3 But know that YHWH has set apart the godly for himself — YHWH hears when I call to him.

4 Be angry, and do not sin — ponder in your own hearts on your beds, and be silent.

5 Offer right sacrifices, and put your trust in YHWH.

6 There are many who say, Who will show us some good? Lift up the light of your face upon us, O YHWH.

7 You have put more joy in my heart than they have when their grain and wine abound.

8 In peace I will both lie down and sleep — for you alone, YHWH, make me dwell in safety.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 4 is attested in 4QPsa with fragmentary coverage. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions. The psalm is marked as an evening psalm by its closing verse — I will lie down and sleep in peace.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 4 in full. The psalm is used in Beta Israel liturgy as an evening prayer.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 4. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — lift up the light of your face upon us, O YHWH. This is the language of the Priestly Blessing of Bamidbar 6:25 — YHWH make his face shine upon you. The Tehillim draws on the covenant blessing formula as both a memory and a petition. Verse 8 — you alone, YHWH, make me dwell in safety. The word alone is the theological center of the verse. Not the walls, not the army, not the grain and wine — YHWH alone is the source of security. This is the monotheistic covenant principle expressed as personal trust in the moment of lying down to sleep.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 5 Of Dawid — Morning Prayer — YHWH Hates the Wicked and Protects the Righteous

1 Give ear to my words, O YHWH — consider my groaning.

2 Give attention to the sound of my cry, my King and my Creator — for to you do I pray.

3 YHWH, in the morning you hear my voice — in the morning I prepare a sacrifice for you and watch.

4 For you are not a Creator who delights in wickedness — evil may not dwell with you.

5 The boastful shall not stand before your eyes — you hate all evildoers.

6 You destroy those who speak lies — YHWH abhors the bloodthirsty and deceitful man.

7 But I, through the abundance of your steadfast love, will enter your house — I will bow down toward your holy temple in the fear of you.

8 Lead me, YHWH, in your righteousness because of my enemies — make your way straight before me.

9 For there is no truth in their mouth — their inmost self is destruction — their throat is an open grave — they flatter with their tongue.

10 Make them bear their guilt, O Creator — let them fall by their own counsels — because of the abundance of their transgressions cast them out, for they have rebelled against you.

11 But let all who take refuge in you rejoice — let them ever sing for joy — and spread your protection over them, that those who love your name may exult in you.

12 For you bless the righteous, YHWH — you cover him with favor as with a shield.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 5 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions. Psalm 5 is the morning counterpart to Psalm 4 as the evening psalm.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 5 in full. The morning orientation of verse 3 — in the morning you hear my voice, in the morning I prepare for you and watch — connects the psalm to the morning liturgy of the covenant community.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 5. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — their throat is an open grave — they flatter with their tongue. The open grave is the image of a throat that receives the living and gives back death — words that appear as welcome but lead to destruction. This verse describes the specific evil of those who appear friendly while plotting harm. Verse 12 — you cover him with favor as with a shield. The Hebrew ratzon — favor, goodwill, delight — is YHWH's disposition toward the righteous. It is a covering, a shield, a surrounding protection. The righteous man walks through a hostile world surrounded by YHWH's favor as armor.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 6 Of Dawid — A Psalm of Deep Distress — Plea for Healing and Deliverance

1 YHWH, rebuke me not in your anger, nor discipline me in your wrath.

2 Be gracious to me, YHWH, for I am languishing — heal me, YHWH, for my bones are troubled.

3 My soul also is greatly troubled — but you, YHWH — how long?

4 Turn, YHWH, deliver my soul — save me for the sake of your steadfast love.

5 For in death there is no remembrance of you — in Sheol who will give you praise?

6 I am weary with my moaning — every night I flood my bed with tears — I drench my couch with my weeping.

7 My eye wastes away because of grief — it grows weak because of all my foes.

8 Depart from me, all you workers of evil — for YHWH has heard the sound of my weeping.

9 YHWH has heard my plea — YHWH accepts my prayer.

10 All my enemies shall be ashamed and greatly troubled — they shall turn back and be put to shame in a moment.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 6 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions. Psalm 6 is the first of the traditional seven penitential psalms.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 6 in full. The psalm is used in Beta Israel liturgy in contexts of illness and communal distress.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 6. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5 — in death there is no remembrance of you — in Sheol who will give you praise? This is a covenant argument directed at YHWH. The psalmist appeals to YHWH's own interest in being praised: if I die, you lose a voice of praise. This is not a theological statement about the afterlife — it is a rhetorical petition grounded in the covenant relationship. YHWH is moved by the praise of his covenant people. Verse 6 — I flood my bed with tears — I drench my couch with my weeping. The physical concreteness of grief in Tehillim is consistent throughout — tears are not merely emotional metaphors but real physical expressions of covenant distress before YHWH.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 7 Of Dawid — A Shiggaion — Plea for Vindication Against the Wicked

1 YHWH my Creator, in you do I take refuge — save me from all my pursuers and deliver me,

2 Lest like a lion they tear my soul apart, rending it in pieces, with none to deliver.

3 YHWH my Creator, if I have done this, if there is wrong in my hands,

4 If I have repaid my friend with evil or plundered my enemy without cause,

5 Let the enemy pursue my soul and overtake it, and let him trample my life to the ground and lay my glory in the dust.

6 Arise, YHWH, in your anger — lift yourself up against the fury of my enemies — awake for me — you have appointed a judgment.

7 Let the assembly of the peoples be gathered about you — over it return on high.

8 YHWH judges the peoples — judge me, YHWH, according to my righteousness and according to the integrity that is in me.

9 Oh, let the evil of the wicked come to an end, and may you establish the righteous — you who test the minds and hearts, O righteous Creator.

10 My shield is with Creator, who saves the upright in heart.

11 Creator is a righteous judge, and a Creator who feels indignation every day.

12 If a man does not repent, Creator will whet his sword — he has bent and readied his bow.

13 He has prepared for him his deadly weapons, making his arrows fiery shafts.

14 Behold, the wicked man conceives evil and is pregnant with mischief and gives birth to lies.

15 He makes a pit, digging it out, and falls into the hole that he has made.

16 His mischief returns upon his own head, and on his own skull his violence descends.

17 I will give to YHWH the thanks due to his righteousness, and I will sing praise to the name of YHWH, the Most High.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 7 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The superscription identifies the psalm as a Shiggaion — a term whose precise meaning is uncertain but may indicate a specific type of lament or musical style. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 7 in full. The psalm is understood as a plea for divine vindication against false accusation — a central theme in the covenant community's theology of justice.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 7. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verses 14-16 — the wicked man conceives evil, is pregnant with mischief, gives birth to lies, digs a pit, and falls into it himself. The extended metaphor of evil as a pregnancy and birth — the wicked giving birth to the very thing that destroys him — is one of the most sophisticated rhetorical constructions in the Tehillim. The pit he digs for others becomes his own grave. His violence descends on his own skull. This is the covenant principle of measure for measure: the evil a man designs for others returns to him precisely because YHWH is a righteous judge who feels indignation every day — verse 11.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 8 Of Dawid — The Majesty of YHWH and the Dignity of Man

1 YHWH, our Sovereign, how majestic is your name in all the earth — you have set your glory above the heavens.

2 Out of the mouth of babies and infants, you have established strength because of your foes, to still the enemy and the avenger.

3 When I look at your heavens, the work of your fingers, the moon and the stars, which you have set in place,

4 What is man that you are mindful of him, and the son of man that you care for him?

5 Yet you have made him a little lower than the heavenly beings, and crowned him with glory and honor.

6 You have given him dominion over the works of your hands — you have put all things under his feet,

7 All sheep and oxen, and also the beasts of the field,

8 The birds of the heavens, and the fish of the sea, whatever passes along the paths of the seas.

9 YHWH, our Sovereign, how majestic is your name in all the earth.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 8 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm opens and closes with the identical refrain — YHWH our Sovereign, how majestic is your name in all the earth — creating a literary frame around the meditation on humanity's place in creation. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 8 in full. The psalm is understood as a meditation on the Bereshit creation account — connecting the dominion given to man in Bereshit 1:28 with the covenant responsibility of humanity as stewards of YHWH's creation.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 8. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4-5 — what is man that you are mindful of him — yet you have made him a little lower than the heavenly beings and crowned him with glory and honor. The tension of Psalm 8 is the tension of Bereshit 1-2: man is infinitesimally small before the cosmos — the moon and stars set in place by YHWH's fingers — yet is simultaneously the crown of creation, given dominion over all. The heavenly beings in verse 5 are the same beings whose existence the entire Qumran corpus affirms — the angelic orders described in Chanokh and the Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice. Man is placed just below that order and just above the animal creation. His dignity is real. His smallness before the Creator is also real. Psalm 8 holds both without collapsing either.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 9 Of Dawid — Thanksgiving for YHWH's Righteous Judgments Over the Nations

1 I will give thanks to YHWH with my whole heart — I will recount all of your wonderful deeds.

2 I will be glad and exult in you — I will sing praise to your name, O Most High.

3 When my enemies turn back, they stumble and perish before your presence.

4 For you have maintained my just cause — you have sat on the throne, giving righteous judgment.

5 You have rebuked the nations — you have made the wicked perish — you have blotted out their name forever and ever.

6 The enemy came to an end in everlasting ruins — their cities you rooted out — the very memory of them has perished.

7 But YHWH sits enthroned forever — he has established his throne for justice,

8 And he judges the world with righteousness — he judges the peoples with uprightness.

9 YHWH is a stronghold for the oppressed, a stronghold in times of trouble.

10 And those who know your name put their trust in you, for you, YHWH, have not forsaken those who seek you.

11 Sing praises to YHWH, who sits enthroned in Tziyon — tell among the peoples his deeds.

12 For he who avenges blood is mindful of them — he does not forget the cry of the afflicted.

13 Be gracious to me, YHWH — see my affliction from those who hate me — O you who lift me up from the gates of death,

14 That I may recount all your praises, that in the gates of the daughter of Tziyon I may rejoice in your salvation.

15 The nations have sunk in the pit that they made — in the net that they hid, their own foot has been caught.

16 YHWH has made himself known — he has executed judgment — the wicked are snared in the work of their own hands.

17 The wicked shall return to Sheol, all the nations that forget Creator.

18 For the needy shall not always be forgotten, and the hope of the poor shall not perish forever.

19 Arise, YHWH — let not man prevail — let the nations be judged before you.

20 Put them in fear, YHWH — let the nations know that they are but men.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 9 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. Psalms 9 and 10 together form an acrostic poem in the Hebrew — their letters follow the aleph-bet in sequence. The DSS tradition preserves them as related but separate psalms. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 9 in full. The psalm is understood as a declaration of YHWH's sovereignty over all nations — a theme central to the Beta Israel covenant theology.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Septuagint combines Psalms 9 and 10 into a single psalm, reflecting an older tradition that recognized their acrostic unity. The DSS and the Masoretic tradition preserve them as two separate psalms, which is the reading followed here.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9-10 — YHWH is a stronghold for the oppressed — those who know your name put their trust in you, for you have not forsaken those who seek you. The covenant name YHWH is the ground of trust. To know the name is not merely intellectual knowledge — it is covenant relationship. Those who know his name put their trust in him because they know the character that name represents: the Creator who hears the cry of the afflicted, who does not forget, who avenges blood, who lifts up from the gates of death. Verse 12 — he who avenges blood is mindful of them — connects directly to Chanokh 47:1, where the blood of the righteous ascends before YHWH of Spirits. The cry of shed innocent blood reaching YHWH is a consistent thread across the entire DSS corpus.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 10 Why Does YHWH Stand Far Away — The Oppressor and the Forgotten Poor

1 Why, YHWH, do you stand far away — why do you hide yourself in times of trouble?

2 In arrogance the wicked hotly pursue the poor — let them be caught in the schemes that they have devised.

3 For the wicked boasts of the desires of his soul, and the one greedy for gain curses and renounces YHWH.

4 In the pride of his face the wicked does not seek him — all his thoughts are, There is no Creator.

5 His ways prosper at all times — your judgments are on high, out of his sight — as for all his foes, he puffs at them.

6 He says in his heart, I shall not be moved — throughout all generations I shall not meet adversity.

7 His mouth is filled with cursing and deceit and oppression — under his tongue are mischief and iniquity.

8 He sits in ambush in the villages — in hiding places he murders the innocent. His eyes stealthily watch for the helpless.

9 He lurks in ambush like a lion in his thicket — he lurks that he may seize the poor — he seizes the poor when he draws him into his net.

10 The helpless are crushed, sink down, and fall by his might.

11 He says in his heart, Creator has forgotten — he has hidden his face — he will never see it.

12 Arise, YHWH — O Creator, lift up your hand — forget not the afflicted.

13 Why does the wicked renounce Creator and say in his heart, You will not call to account?

14 But you do see — for you note mischief and vexation, that you may take it into your hands. To you the helpless commits himself — you have been the helper of the fatherless.

15 Break the arm of the wicked and evildoer — call his wickedness to account till you find none.

16 YHWH is King forever and ever — the nations perish from his land.

17 YHWH, you hear the desire of the afflicted — you will strengthen their heart — you will incline your ear

18 To do justice to the fatherless and the oppressed, so that man who is of the earth may strike terror no more.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 10 is the continuation of the acrostic begun in Psalm 9. It is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 10 in full. The psalm's sustained portrait of the wicked — his theology, his speech, his behavior toward the poor — is read as a description of the adversary of the covenant community in every generation.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Septuagint combines Psalms 9 and 10 as a single psalm. The DSS and Masoretic preserve them as two separate psalms. No significant internal variants from the DSS in Psalm 10.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — all his thoughts are, There is no Creator. The practical atheism of the wicked — not philosophical denial but operational dismissal of YHWH from consideration in daily life. He does not deny YHWH's existence in theory; he acts as though YHWH does not see or judge. Verse 11 — Creator has forgotten — he has hidden his face — he will never see it. This is the accusation the wicked makes against YHWH. Psalm 10 then turns to address YHWH directly: but you do see — verse 14. The entire psalm moves from the complaint that YHWH is far away — verse 1 — to the declaration that YHWH does see and does hear — verse 17. The resolution is not the removal of the wicked in the psalm — it is the affirmation of YHWH's knowledge and ultimate sovereignty.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 11 Of Dawid — Trust in YHWH When Foundations Are Destroyed

1 In YHWH I take refuge — how can you say to my soul, Flee like a bird to your mountain,

2 For behold, the wicked bend the bow — they have fitted their arrow to the string to shoot in the dark at the upright in heart.

3 If the foundations are destroyed, what can the righteous do?

4 YHWH is in his holy temple — YHWH's throne is in heaven — his eyes see — his eyelids test the children of man.

5 YHWH tests the righteous, but his soul hates the wicked and the one who loves violence.

6 Let him rain coals on the wicked — fire and sulfur and a scorching wind shall be the portion of their cup.

7 For YHWH is righteous — he loves righteous deeds — the upright shall behold his face.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 11 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 11 in full. The psalm's central question — if the foundations are destroyed, what can the righteous do? — is read as the perennial question of the covenant community under persecution.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 11. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — if the foundations are destroyed, what can the righteous do? This is the question of the despairing counselor — the voice that says flee. The psalmist refuses to flee. His answer is in verse 4: YHWH is in his holy temple — YHWH's throne is in heaven. The foundations that appear destroyed are earthly social and political structures. The foundation that cannot be destroyed is YHWH's throne. Verse 7 — the upright shall behold his face. This is the ultimate covenant promise — not merely safety or prosperity but face-to-face presence with YHWH. The highest expression of covenant blessing in the Torah — the Priestly Blessing of Bamidbar 6 — is that YHWH would lift his face upon you. Psalm 11 closes with the fulfillment of that promise.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 12 Of Dawid — The Godly Have Vanished — The Pure Words of YHWH

1 Save, YHWH, for the godly one is gone — for the faithful have vanished from among the children of man.

2 Everyone utters lies to his neighbor — with flattering lips and a double heart they speak.

3 May YHWH cut off all flattering lips, the tongue that makes great boasts,

4 Those who say, With our tongue we will prevail — our lips are our own — who is master over us?

5 Because the poor are plundered, because the needy groan, I will now arise, says YHWH — I will place him in the safety for which he longs.

6 The words of YHWH are pure words, like silver refined in a furnace on the ground, purified seven times.

7 You, YHWH, will keep them — you will guard us from this generation forever.

8 On every side the wicked prowl, as vileness is exalted among the children of man.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 12 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 12 in full. The contrast between the corrupt speech of men — flattering lips, double heart, boastful tongue — and the pure words of YHWH purified seven times is read as the foundational distinction between human and divine speech.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 12. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — the words of YHWH are pure words, like silver refined in a furnace on the ground, purified seven times. This is placed in deliberate contrast with the corrupt speech described in verses 2-4. The double heart — literally, a heart and a heart — means a heart that says one thing while concealing another. Against this YHWH's words are presented as pure silver — refined seven times, which is the number of completeness in the Hebrew covenant system. YHWH's speech is absolutely reliable. His word is his covenant. The seven-fold refining is the covenant's integrity.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 13 Of Dawid — How Long, YHWH — Darkness Turning to Trust

1 How long, YHWH — will you forget me forever — how long will you hide your face from me?

2 How long must I take counsel in my soul and have sorrow in my heart all the day — how long shall my enemy be exalted over me?

3 Consider and answer me, YHWH my Creator — light up my eyes, lest I sleep the sleep of death,

4 Lest my enemy say, I have prevailed over him — lest my foes rejoice because I am shaken.

5 But I have trusted in your steadfast love — my heart shall rejoice in your salvation.

6 I will sing to YHWH, because he has dealt bountifully with me.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 13 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions. Psalm 13 is one of the most compressed lament psalms in the collection — six verses moving from anguish to trust.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 13 in full. The fourfold How long — verses 1-2 — is read as the paradigmatic voice of the covenant community in seasons of prolonged suffering when YHWH appears absent.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 13. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The movement of Psalm 13 is one of the most dramatic in the Tehillim. Verses 1-2: four cries of How long — the psalmist in full darkness. Verses 3-4: a direct petition — answer me, light up my eyes. Verses 5-6: a sudden turn to trust and praise — I have trusted in your steadfast love — I will sing to YHWH. Nothing external has changed between verse 4 and verse 5. The enemy has not been defeated. The situation has not resolved. The shift is entirely internal — a choice of trust in the middle of darkness. This is the covenant pattern: not waiting for resolution before praising, but praising as an act of covenant faithfulness before resolution comes.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 14 Of Dawid — The Fool Says There Is No Creator — Corruption and the Hope of Yisra'EL

1 The fool says in his heart, There is no Creator. They are corrupt, they do abominable deeds — there is none who does good.

2 YHWH looks down from heaven on the children of man to see if there are any who understand, who seek after Creator.

3 They have all turned aside — together they have become corrupt — there is none who does good, not even one.

4 Have they no knowledge, all the evildoers who eat up my people as they eat bread and do not call upon YHWH?

5 There they are in great terror, for Creator is with the generation of the righteous.

6 You would shame the plans of the poor, but YHWH is his refuge.

7 Oh, that salvation for Yisra'EL would come out of Tziyon — when YHWH restores the fortunes of his people, let Yaakov rejoice, let Yisra'EL be glad.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 14 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. Psalm 14 and Psalm 53 are near-identical, with Psalm 53 using Elohim where Psalm 14 uses YHWH. Both are preserved in the DSS tradition. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 14 in full. The universal declaration that none does good — none who seeks Creator — is read not as absolute despair but as the covenant context that makes YHWH's salvation necessary.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text of Psalm 14 aligns with the DSS reading. The near-duplicate Psalm 53 preserves an alternate version using Elohim as the divine name. In this compilation YHWH is the reading throughout, as preserved in the primary DSS tradition.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — the fool says in his heart, There is no Creator. The Hebrew naval — fool — is not an intellectual category but a moral one. The fool is not an atheist in the philosophical sense — he is someone who lives and acts as though YHWH does not see or judge. This connects directly to Psalm 10:4 where the wicked's thoughts are, there is no Creator. The practical dismissal of YHWH from daily moral consideration is the defining characteristic of the fool and the wicked throughout the Tehillim. Verse 7 — oh, that salvation for Yisra'EL would come out of Tziyon — the closing eschatological cry. Even in a psalm of universal human corruption, the horizon is the restoration of YHWH's people. The covenant hope persists through the darkest anthropology.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 15 Of Dawid — Who May Dwell on YHWH's Holy Hill — The Covenant Character

1 YHWH, who shall sojourn in your tent — who shall dwell on your holy hill?

2 He who walks blamelessly and does what is right and speaks truth in his heart,

3 Who does not slander with his tongue and does no evil to his neighbor, nor takes up a reproach against his friend,

4 In whose eyes a vile person is despised, but who honors those who fear YHWH — who swears to his own hurt and does not change,

5 Who does not put out his money at interest and does not take a bribe against the innocent. He who does these things shall never be moved.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 15 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions. Psalm 15 is a torah-psalm — a psalm that defines covenant character through a catalog of behaviors.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 15 in full. The psalm is read as a covenant entrance liturgy — the question of who may enter YHWH's presence answered through a description of the covenant character rather than a ritual qualification.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 15. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 15 answers the question of covenant access not with ritual requirements but with ethical ones. The person who may dwell on YHWH's holy hill is defined by integrity in speech — no slander, no false oath — integrity in relationships — no evil to neighbor, no reproach against friend — integrity in economics — no usury, no bribery. Verse 4 — who swears to his own hurt and does not change. The covenant person keeps his oath even when keeping it costs him. This is the covenant character of YHWH himself — whose steadfast love endures even when the covenant partner fails. Psalm 15 calls the covenant people to embody the character of their Creator.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 16 A Miktam of Dawid — YHWH Is My Chosen Portion and My Cup

1 Preserve me, O Creator, for in you I take refuge.

2 I say to YHWH, You are my Sovereign — I have no good apart from you.

3 As for the holy ones in the land, they are the excellent ones in whom is all my delight.

4 The sorrows of those who run after another god shall multiply — their drink offerings of blood I will not pour out or take their names on my lips.

5 YHWH is my chosen portion and my cup — you hold my lot.

6 The lines have fallen for me in pleasant places — indeed, I have a beautiful inheritance.

7 I bless YHWH who gives me counsel — in the night also my heart instructs me.

8 I have set YHWH always before me — because he is at my right hand, I shall not be shaken.

9 Therefore my heart is glad, and my whole being rejoices — my flesh also dwells secure.

10 For you will not abandon my soul to Sheol, or let your holy one see corruption.

11 You make known to me the path of life — in your presence there is fullness of joy — at your right hand are pleasures forevermore.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 16 is well attested in 4QPsc (4Q85) which preserves this psalm in Aramaic alongside the Hebrew — one of the very few psalms found in Aramaic at Qumran. This confirms the psalm's significance in the Qumran community's theological reflection. Verse 10 — you will not abandon my soul to Sheol — is particularly significant in the DSS eschatological context.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 16 in full. The psalm is read as a declaration of covenant sufficiency — YHWH as the only necessary inheritance — and as a statement of confidence in resurrection beyond death.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 16. The Aramaic Targum of this psalm and its appearance in Aramaic at Qumran both confirm the antiquity and canonical standing of the text.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10 — you will not abandon my soul to Sheol, or let your holy one see corruption. This is one of the most theologically significant verses in the Tehillim in the DSS context. The covenant confidence that YHWH will not abandon the righteous to Sheol permanently is the foundation of the resurrection theology found in the Epistle of Chanokh and the War Scroll. Verse 11 — in your presence there is fullness of joy — at your right hand are pleasures forevermore. The highest expression of covenant blessing is not land or children or long life — it is presence with YHWH. The path of life leads not to a destination but to a relationship.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 17 A Prayer of Dawid — Hear a Just Cause — Guard Me as the Apple of Your Eye

1 Hear a just cause, YHWH — attend to my cry — give ear to my prayer from lips free of deceit.

2 From your presence let my vindication come — let your eyes behold the right.

3 You have tried my heart, you have visited me by night, you have tested me, and you will find nothing — I have purposed that my mouth will not transgress.

4 With regard to the works of man, by the word of your lips I have avoided the ways of the violent.

5 My steps have held fast to your paths — my feet have not slipped.

6 I call upon you, for you will answer me, O Creator — incline your ear to me, hear my words.

7 Wondrously show your steadfast love, O Savior of those who seek refuge from their adversaries at your right hand.

8 Keep me as the apple of your eye — hide me in the shadow of your wings,

9 From the wicked who do me violence, my deadly enemies who surround me.

10 They close their hearts to pity — with their mouths they speak arrogantly.

11 They have now surrounded our steps — they set their eyes to cast us to the ground.

12 He is like a lion eager to tear, as a young lion lurking in ambush.

13 Arise, YHWH — confront him, subdue him — deliver my soul from the wicked by your sword,

14 From men by your hand, YHWH, from men of the world whose portion is in this life. You fill their womb with treasure — they are satisfied with children, and they leave their abundance to their infants.

15 As for me, I shall behold your face in righteousness — when I awake, I shall be satisfied with your likeness.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 17 is attested in 8QPsa (8Q2) from Cave 8 which preserves Psalms 17 and 18. This is a significant confirmation — Cave 8 is one of the smaller caves whose manuscript witnesses are particularly valuable. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 17 in full. The prayer of the innocent man before YHWH who tests the heart by night is read as the covenant paradigm of righteousness vindicated by the one who truly knows the interior life.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 17. The Cave 8 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — keep me as the apple of your eye — hide me in the shadow of your wings. Two protective images. The apple of the eye — the most sensitive and carefully guarded part of the body — is also used in Devarim 32:10 where YHWH guards Yisra'EL as the apple of his eye in the wilderness. The shadow of your wings appears again in Psalm 36:7, 57:1, 61:4, and 63:7 — the image of the great bird sheltering her young. Verse 15 — when I awake, I shall be satisfied with your likeness. The wicked are satisfied with earthly treasure and children — verse 14. The psalmist is satisfied with beholding YHWH's face. The awakening may be the awakening from sleep or the awakening of resurrection — both are present in the DSS reading of this verse.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 18 Of Dawid — A Song of Deliverance — The Great Theophany Psalm

1 I love you, YHWH, my strength.

2 YHWH is my rock and my fortress and my deliverer, my Creator, my rock, in whom I take refuge, my shield, and the horn of my salvation, my stronghold.

3 I call upon YHWH, who is worthy to be praised, and I am saved from my enemies.

4 The cords of death encompassed me — the torrents of destruction assailed me.

5 The cords of Sheol entangled me — the snares of death confronted me.

6 In my distress I called upon YHWH — to my Creator I cried for help. From his temple he heard my voice, and my cry to him reached his ears.

7 Then the earth reeled and rocked — the foundations also of the mountains trembled and quaked, because he was angry.

8 Smoke went up from his nostrils, and devouring fire from his mouth — glowing coals flamed forth from him.

9 He bowed the heavens and came down — thick darkness was under his feet.

10 He rode on a cherub and flew — he came swiftly on the wings of the wind.

11 He made darkness his covering, his canopy around him, thick clouds dark with water.

12 Out of the brightness before him hailstones and coals of fire broke through his clouds.

13 YHWH also thundered in the heavens, and the Most High uttered his voice — hailstones and coals of fire.

14 And he sent out his arrows and scattered them — he flashed forth lightnings and routed them.

15 Then the channels of the sea were seen, and the foundations of the world were laid bare at your rebuke, YHWH, at the blast of the breath of your nostrils.

16 He sent from on high, he took me — he drew me out of many waters.

17 He rescued me from my strong enemy and from those who hated me, for they were too mighty for me.

18 They confronted me in the day of my calamity, but YHWH was my support.

19 He brought me out into a broad place — he rescued me, because he delighted in me.

20 YHWH dealt with me according to my righteousness — according to the cleanness of my hands he rewarded me.

21 For I have kept the ways of YHWH and have not wickedly departed from my Creator.

22 For all his rules were before me, and his statutes I did not put away from me.

23 I was blameless before him, and I kept myself from my guilt.

24 So YHWH has rewarded me according to my righteousness, according to the cleanness of my hands in his sight.

25 With the merciful you show yourself merciful — with the blameless man you show yourself blameless —

26 With the purified you show yourself pure — and with the crooked you make yourself seem tortuous.

27 For you save a humble people, but the haughty eyes you bring down.

28 For it is you who light my lamp — YHWH my Creator lightens my darkness.

29 For by you I can run against a troop, and by my Creator I can leap over a wall.

30 This Creator — his way is perfect — the word of YHWH proves true — he is a shield for all those who take refuge in him.

31 For who is Creator, except YHWH — and who is a rock, except our Creator?

32 This Creator who equipped me with strength and made my way blameless.

33 He made my feet like the feet of a deer and set me secure on the heights.

34 He trains my hands for war, so that my arms can bend a bow of bronze.

35 You have given me the shield of your salvation, and your right hand supported me, and your gentleness made me great.

36 You gave a wide place for my steps under me, and my feet did not slip.

37 I pursued my enemies and overtook them, and did not turn back till they were consumed.

38 I thrust them through, so that they were not able to rise — they fell under my feet.

39 For you equipped me with strength for the battle — you made those who rise against me sink under me.

40 You made my enemies turn their backs to me, and those who hated me I destroyed.

41 They cried for help, but there was none to save — they cried to YHWH, but he did not answer them.

42 I beat them fine as dust before the wind — I cast them out like the mire of the streets.

43 You delivered me from strife with the people — you made me the head of the nations — people whom I had not known served me.

44 As soon as they heard of me they obeyed me — foreigners came cringing to me.

45 Foreigners lost heart and came trembling out of their fortresses.

46 YHWH lives, and blessed be my rock, and exalted be the Creator of my salvation —

47 The Creator who gave me vengeance and subdued peoples under me,

48 Who delivered me from my enemies — yes, you exalted me above those who rose against me — you rescued me from the man of violence.

49 For this I will praise you, YHWH, among the nations, and sing to your name.

50 Great salvation he brings to his king, and shows steadfast love to his anointed, to Dawid and his offspring forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 18 is preserved in 8QPsa (8Q2) from Cave 8 alongside Psalm 17. This is one of the longest psalms in Book I and also appears in 2 Shemu'el 22 as the Song of Dawid — making it one of the best-attested poems in the entire Hebrew corpus. 4QPsc (4Q85) also preserves portions of this psalm. The DSS text shows minor variants from the 2 Shemu'el 22 version, confirming that the two versions represent a common tradition with independent transmission histories.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 18 in full. The great theophany — YHWH descending on the cherub, riding on the wings of the wind, the earth rocking and the channels of the sea exposed — is read as the archetypal divine intervention on behalf of the righteous king.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text of Psalm 18 has minor variants from the parallel text in 2 Shemu'el 22. The DSS readings generally support the Psalm 18 version over the Shemu'el version where they differ, confirming the Psalm version as the primary liturgical text.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verses 7-15 contain one of the most dramatic theophany descriptions in the entire Hebrew canon. YHWH descends from heaven in cosmic fury — earth trembles, smoke rises, devouring fire, thick darkness, cherub, wings of the wind, hailstones, lightning, the channels of the sea laid bare. This is the same warrior-deity language found in Shemot 15 — the Song of the Sea — and in Devarim 33 — the Blessing of Moshe. YHWH is not a distant philosophical deity. In the ancient Hebrew covenant framework he rides the storm, bends the heavens, scatters enemies with arrows of lightning. Verse 50 — he shows steadfast love to his Anointed, to Dawid and his offspring forever — closes the psalm with the Davidic covenant of 2 Shemu'el 7 as its theological anchor.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 19 Of Dawid — The Heavens Declare — The Torah of YHWH Is Perfect

1 The heavens declare the glory of Creator, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork.

2 Day to day pours out speech, and night to night reveals knowledge.

3 There is no speech, nor are there words, whose voice is not heard.

4 Their voice goes out through all the earth, and their words to the end of the world. In them he has set a tent for the sun,

5 Which comes out like a bridegroom leaving his chamber, and, like a strong man, runs its course with joy.

6 Its rising is from the end of the heavens, and its circuit to the end of them, and there is nothing hidden from its heat.

7 The Torah of YHWH is perfect, reviving the soul — the testimony of YHWH is sure, making wise the simple.

8 The precepts of YHWH are right, rejoicing the heart — the commandment of YHWH is pure, enlightening the eyes.

9 The fear of YHWH is clean, enduring forever — the rules of YHWH are true, and righteous altogether.

10 More to be desired are they than gold, even much fine gold — sweeter also than honey and drippings of the honeycomb.

11 Moreover, by them is your servant warned — in keeping them there is great reward.

12 Who can discern his errors — declare me innocent from hidden faults.

13 Keep back your servant also from presumptuous sins — let them not have dominion over me. Then I shall be blameless, and innocent of great transgression.

14 Let the words of my mouth and the meditation of my heart be acceptable in your sight, YHWH, my rock and my redeemer.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 19 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm is structurally a diptych — the first section (verses 1-6) meditates on the silent testimony of the created cosmos, the second (verses 7-14) on the Torah of YHWH. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 19 in full. The connection between creation and Torah — both declaring YHWH's glory, both carrying his instruction — is central to the Beta Israel theological tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 19. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 19 is the Tehillim's great statement that creation and Torah are two forms of the same revelation. The heavens speak without words — verse 3 — and the Torah speaks with words. Both reveal YHWH. The connection to the Astronomical Book of Chanokh is direct: the sun running its course, the circuit of the heavens, the established tent — these are the same ordered celestial movements that Chanokh received from Uriel and transmitted to Metushelakh. The covenant calendar of the Qumran community — the 364-day solar year — is grounded in exactly this conviction that the heavenly circuits are YHWH's declaration. Verse 14 — let the words of my mouth and the meditation of my heart be acceptable in your sight, YHWH, my rock and my redeemer — is one of the most beloved closing verses in all of Tehillim, traditionally used to close prayer.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 20 Of Dawid — A Prayer for the King Before Battle — Trust in YHWH Not in Chariots

1 May YHWH answer you in the day of trouble — may the name of the Creator of Yaakov protect you.

2 May he send you help from the sanctuary and give you support from Tziyon.

3 May he remember all your offerings and regard with favor your burnt sacrifices.

4 May he grant you your heart's desire and fulfill all your plans.

5 May we shout for joy over your salvation, and in the name of our Creator set up our banners — may YHWH fulfill all your petitions.

6 Now I know that YHWH saves his anointed — he will answer him from his holy heaven with the saving might of his right hand.

7 Some trust in chariots and some in horses, but we trust in the name of YHWH our Creator.

8 They collapse and fall, but we rise and stand upright.

9 YHWH, save the king — may he answer us when we call.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 20 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm is a communal prayer for the king before battle — the congregation praying on behalf of the Anointed. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 20 in full. The psalm is read as a liturgical prayer for the covenant king — connecting the earthly king's battle success to YHWH's saving action from his heavenly sanctuary.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 20. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — some trust in chariots and some in horses, but we trust in the name of YHWH our Creator. This is the covenant principle of Devarim 17:16 where the king is forbidden to multiply horses for himself. The prohibition is not against horses per se but against the political and military theology that makes military hardware the ground of security rather than YHWH. The War Scroll's cavalry regulations exist within this same framework — the cavalry serves YHWH's war, not the war that replaces YHWH. Verse 5 — in the name of our Creator set up our banners — is the exact language of the War Scroll Column III, where every battle banner carries YHWH's name. The War Scroll is a practical application of the theology of Psalm 20.

END OF PART ONE — PSALMS 1 THROUGH 20
